lens.included_activities: US lower-middle-market manufacturers of envelopes, paper office supplies, pads, notebooks, and related stationery that repeatedly supply external business, institutional, wholesale, or private-label customers under recurring orders or programs.
lens.excluded_activities: Consumer-brand-only stationery businesses without repeat outsourced production relationships; captive plants; commodity paper mills; pure distributors; printing businesses classified outside 322230; shells and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Primarily per-unit product revenue through repeat purchase orders, annual supply arrangements, private-label programs, and recurring B2B or institutional accounts; some work is custom-converting or specification-driven rather than sold as a standardized retail product.
lens.deviation_from_default: The code is heterogeneous between repeat B2B outsourced production and consumer/commodity stationery. The lens keeps only manufacturers whose external customers repeatedly outsource specification, conversion, or supply responsibility, because applying a service-transfer screen to retail-brand product sales would be incoherent.

a.bridge: O*NET identifies the core paper-goods occupation as physical machine setup, control, defect inspection, and adjustment, while the cross-economy GPT study finds broad exposure concentrated in language and information tasks. I map low exposure to direct converting labor and higher exposure to quoting, scheduling, purchasing, customer service, artwork/specification handling, documentation, and quality administration. The national GPT benchmark likely overstates this production-heavy industry's wage-weighted exposure; the O*NET occupation is broader than stationery and does not provide a 322230 wage share.
a.rationale: Most shop-floor converting and material handling remains physical, but a meaningful minority of wages sits in information-rich production support and commercial workflows. AI can draft quotes and work instructions, normalize customer specifications, forecast demand, schedule jobs, triage quality records, automate order entry, and reduce administrative hiring. The range excludes tasks already automated by conventional machinery and ERP rules.
a.change_evidence: A 322230-specific payroll-by-occupation extract from BLS OEWS/QCEW microdata, paired with time-and-motion interviews at at least ten envelope, notebook, and office-supply plants and a task-level inventory of current ERP/automation use.
caveats: No public source directly measures not-yet-automated AI task exposure for NAICS 322230.
The available occupation evidence covers converted paper products or the occupation nationally, not the six-digit stationery industry.
Small plants may combine sales, scheduling, and supervision in owner-operators, making wage weights unstable.

rho.bridge: The 2021 Census manufacturing survey measured any industrial AI use at 22.8% of US plants, and broader Census/Federal Reserve monitoring reports about 18% of firms had adopted AI by year-end 2025. These measure adoption, not the fraction of exposed opportunity implemented within five years. I use them as a floor/context and raise the five-year range for increasingly packaged ERP, vision, forecasting, and document tools, while discounting for small-firm integration, data quality, change management, and physical-process constraints. Broad firm adoption may overstate deep workflow implementation.
rho.rationale: Standardized order, planning, customer-service, and document workflows are implementable with commercially available tools, but fragmented legacy systems, short production runs, scarce technical staff, and quality accountability slow full deployment. Implementation should be materially higher than current observed plant use yet well below complete realization of exposed tasks.
rho.change_evidence: A structured survey of 322230 plants documenting deployed use cases, labor hours before and after deployment, ERP/MES readiness, pilot-to-production conversion, exception rates, and implementation payback by plant size.
caveats: Industrial AI adoption includes technologies beyond generative AI and may include shallow use.
The adoption evidence covers all manufacturing or all firms, not stationery manufacturers or LMM firms specifically.
Five-year diffusion is sensitive to vendor packaging and plant data quality.

e.rationale: The NAICS definition spans envelopes, die-cut office supplies, and stationery. A substantial but not dominant share of LMM firms should have repeat B2B, institutional, wholesale, or private-label programs that fit the outsourced recurring-service lens. Consumer brands, commodity sellers, captive operations, and firms without durable external account relationships are excluded.
e.change_evidence: A firm-level census of the 99 dataset-estimated LMM firms using Capital IQ, PitchBook, company websites, customer concentration disclosures, and management interviews to classify revenue as repeat B2B/private-label versus retail or transactional product sales.
caveats: Eligibility is not reported in public industry statistics and requires firm-by-firm revenue-model classification.
The frozen n estimate is margin-bridged and may misstate the number of firms in the EBITDA band.
Repeat product orders are treated as outsourced production only when the manufacturer retains accountable fulfillment responsibility.

s5.bridge: Census reports that over half of US business owners were age 55 or older, while BizBuySell observes completed manufacturing transactions and reported a 16% year-over-year rise in Q1 2026 manufacturing acquisitions. These establish succession pressure and an active small-manufacturing sale market but do not measure a five-year control-transfer probability for eligible 322230 firms. I translate age pressure and observed liquidity into an annualized five-year judgment, discounting internal reorganizations, closures, and nontransferable owner-dependent firms. The proxy may overstate transfers because sale-platform transactions are selected successes.
s5.rationale: Aging ownership supports availability, and manufacturing businesses demonstrably transact, but only a fraction of eligible firms will complete a qualifying control transfer within five years. Asset intensity and customer qualification can help transferability, while declining demand, environmental liabilities, customer concentration, and owner dependence can instead cause closure or failed processes.
s5.change_evidence: Deal-log data from GF Data, PitchBook, Axial, BizBuySell, SBA 7(a) change-of-ownership loans, and at least five paper-products brokers, filtered to 322230-like businesses and reconciled to the eligible-firm denominator.
caveats: Owner-age evidence covers all US employer businesses, not this industry or EBITDA band.
BizBuySell covers mostly Main Street transactions and may miss larger LMM deals and private transfers.
A leadership succession is not necessarily a qualifying control transfer.

q.rationale: Per-unit and program pricing lets an operator retain workflow savings between bids and repricing events, particularly in custom, short-run, or specification-heavy accounts. Retention erodes as annual negotiations, indexed material clauses, competitive rebids, and customer procurement teams discover lower costs. Savings tied to faster response, fewer errors, and better service are more defensible than pure direct-labor reductions.
q.change_evidence: Customer-contract review across at least 30 accounts covering price-adjustment clauses, bid frequency, open-book costing, renewal cadence, customer concentration, and realized gross-margin changes after documented automation projects.
caveats: No public evidence isolates contractual pass-through of AI savings in stationery manufacturing.
Paper input costs can dominate price discussions and obscure labor-productivity capture.
Large office-supply and institutional buyers may have stronger repricing leverage than the base case assumes.

d5.bridge: USPS OIG projects 2025-2035 First-Class and Marketing Mail volume declines of 14% to 41%, and USPS reports total FY2025 mail volume of 108.7 billion pieces. Mail volume is a direct demand driver for envelopes but not notebooks, pads, art stationery, or all office supplies. I apply a milder five-year decline to the mixed current basket because non-mail stationery can be steadier and premium/creative segments may grow. The mail proxy likely overstates decline for non-envelope products but may understate digital substitution in traditional office paper.
d5.rationale: Digital billing, communication, filing, and school/office workflows continue to reduce traditional paper demand, especially envelopes and machine paper. Physical education, legal/compliance uses, direct mail, journaling, crafts, premium stationery, and recurring institutional requirements prevent collapse. The base implies continued real contraction rather than disappearance.
d5.change_evidence: Constant-dollar 322230 shipment history from the Economic Census/AIES by product class, customer-channel sell-through from Circana or NIQ, and five-year volume forecasts from major envelope and office-supply customers separated into mail, education, office, and consumer uses.
caveats: USPS mail volume is only a proxy for the envelope portion of this mixed industry.
Nominal shipment data require product-level deflation and are not used directly in the range.
Demand could diverge sharply between commodity office products and premium consumer stationery.

o.rationale: For demand that remains in year five, customers still need an accountable manufacturer to procure substrates, convert material, maintain equipment, inspect output, meet specifications, and deliver physical goods. Software can eliminate some ordering and design intermediation and enable self-service customization, but it cannot supply the physical basket without an operator. Contract manufacturers with reliable quality and fulfillment remain necessary.
o.change_evidence: Customer interviews and channel data measuring migration to self-serve print-on-demand platforms, in-house converting, direct imports, or software-only substitutes, plus win/loss analyses identifying whether the accountable manufacturer role was removed or merely changed supplier.
caveats: This estimates operator requirement conditional on surviving demand, not total demand durability.
Large customers may vertically integrate or source directly from offshore plants.
Online configuration can reduce sales and prepress labor without eliminating manufacturing accountability.

narrative.executive_view: The coherent opportunity is not generic stationery brands but repeat B2B and institutional manufacturers that own specification, conversion, quality, and fulfillment. AI can improve commercial and production-support workflows, yet physical converting dominates the core work and the current product basket faces secular digitization pressure.
narrative.ai_impact: Near-term gains concentrate in quote generation, order entry, specification parsing, artwork checks, demand forecasting, production scheduling, purchasing, customer service, maintenance triage, and quality documentation. Machine tending, material movement, setup, and physical defect correction remain constrained by equipment and robotics rather than language models alone.
narrative.value_capture: Fixed per-unit and program prices allow temporary retention of labor and error-reduction savings. Capture is stronger where faster turnaround and customization matter, and weaker in commodity bids, open-book accounts, or annual procurement rebids where customers can force pass-through.
narrative.firm_availability: Aging US business ownership and an active market for small manufacturing transactions support a plausible acquisition pipeline. Actual eligible supply is narrower because many firms are consumer brands, transactional commodity sellers, owner-dependent shops, or too exposed to declining legacy products.
narrative.demand_durability: Envelopes and office paper face ongoing electronic substitution, corroborated by USPS's long-run mail decline. Remaining physical demand in education, compliance, direct mail, art, journaling, and premium products still requires accountable manufacturing, but product and customer mix will determine whether a firm contracts or remains stable.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the six-digit occupation mix, the share of firms with genuine repeat outsourced relationships, product-level real demand, customer concentration, and whether legacy systems support implementation. Paper-price volatility, tariffs, equipment capex, environmental obligations, and procurement-led repricing could overwhelm AI savings. A business concentrated in commodity envelopes or machine paper with annual rebids could be a poor acquisition despite operational upside.
narrative.principal_driver: Repeat specification-driven B2B production creates automatable information workflows around a still-essential physical fulfillment role.
narrative.principal_weakness: Secular digitization pressures core envelope and office-paper volumes while most direct production labor remains physically bound.

sources.title: 322230: Stationery Product Manufacturing - Census Bureau Profile
sources.supports: Defines the industry as converting paper or paperboard into writing, filing, artwork, envelope, and similar stationery products and lists representative activities.

sources.title: Converted Paper Product Manufacturing - May 2023 OEWS Industry-Specific Estimates
sources.supports: Shows paper-goods machine setters/operators/tenders as a major converted-paper occupation, with 63,660 workers and 23.32% of industry employment at the four-digit level.

sources.title: O*NET: Paper Goods Machine Setters, Operators, and Tenders
sources.supports: Documents physical core tasks including machine control, tension and speed adjustment, finished-product inspection, and correction of production problems.

sources.title: GPTs are GPTs: An Early Look at the Labor Market Impact Potential of Large Language Models
sources.supports: Reports that about 80% of the US workforce could have at least 10% of tasks affected and about 19% could have at least 50% affected; used only as a cross-economy exposure proxy.

sources.title: The Adoption of Industrial AI in America
sources.supports: Reports from a purpose-designed Census survey of about 28,500 US manufacturing establishments that 22.8% of plants used any AI in 2021, with lower intensity-weighted adoption.

sources.title: Monitoring AI Adoption in the US Economy
sources.supports: Summarizes Census business surveys indicating about 18% of firms had adopted AI by year-end 2025 and provides broader workforce adoption context.

sources.title: 2016 Manufacturing and International Trade Report
sources.supports: Reports 2016 stationery-products shipments of $5.696 billion, including $2.268 billion of commercial envelopes and $1.828 billion of business-machine and miscellaneous office paper, demonstrating product-mix heterogeneity.

sources.title: Annual Business Survey Visualizations
sources.supports: Census states that more than half of US business owners were age 55 or older in the 2019 Annual Business Survey, supporting broad succession pressure.

sources.title: BizBuySell Insight Report - Market Trends
sources.supports: Reports Q1 2026 manufacturing acquisitions up 16% year over year and 22% quarter over quarter, evidencing an active but selected small-business transaction market.

sources.title: Projecting Mail Volume: Future Trends and Implications for the Postal Service
sources.supports: States that mail volume peaked in 2006 due largely to digital alternatives and projects a further 14% to 41% decline in First-Class and Marketing Mail from 2025 to 2035.

sources.title: USPS Postal Facts: Total Mail Volume
sources.supports: Reports total fiscal-year 2025 US mail volume of 108.7 billion pieces, grounding the scale of the envelope-demand proxy.
